---
title: "Systemic Shadow Prices for Transboundary Ecosystem Services: An Environmentally Extended Multi-Regional Input-Output Approach for the European Union"
author:
  - name: Nils Droste
    affiliation: University of Copenhagen
    email: nd@ifro.ku.dk
date: today
abstract: 
  This paper develops a systemic framework for computing shadow prices of ecosystem services (ES) across European Union member states. Conventional valuation methods based on willingness-to-pay surveys reflect consumer preferences but fail to capture the macroeconomic opportunity costs of ecosystem degradation across integrated supply chains. We integrate the United Nations System of Environmental-Economic Accounting — Ecosystem Accounting (SEEA EA) with an environmentally extended multi-regional input-output (EE-MRIO) model, augmented by stock-flow consistent (SFC) macroeconomic dynamics. Shadow prices are derived as the solution to the Leontief price dual, where the ecosystem valuation vector is set equal to the marginal abatement cost of restoring degraded assets to their SEEA EA baseline stocks. Using FIGARO-REG data covering 10 macro-sectors across 288 NUTS2 regions in 29 EU countries (2017 base year), linked to INCA ecosystem service accounts for nine ES types, we find that supply-chain-embedded ecosystem shadow costs amount to approximately €1.83 trillion (12% of EU GDP), with a mean supply-chain amplification factor of approximately 1,000 relative to direct attribution. Of this, €50 billion represents transboundary shadow costs — ecosystem maintenance obligations that cross national borders through supply-chain linkages — establishing a quantitative lower bound for the fiscal externality that ecological fiscal transfer mechanisms should address. Our results have direct implications for EU green accounting standards, the calibration of ecological fiscal transfers, and the design of nature-based solution markets.

keywords: [ecosystem services, shadow pricing, SEEA EA, multi-regional input-output, stock-flow consistency, European Union, environmental accounting]

jel: [Q51, Q57, F18, E17, C67]
---

```{julia}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
using Pkg
Pkg.activate(joinpath(@__DIR__, ".."))
include(joinpath(@__DIR__, "..", "src", "EUSEEAShadow.jl"))
using .EUSEEAShadow
```

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

Ecosystems do not respect political borders. A forest in the Carpathians regulates water for downstream agriculture in Hungary; Spanish olive groves sequester carbon consumed by German manufacturing chains; Danish coastal wetlands buffer storm surges whose beneficiaries span the North Sea. Yet conventional environmental accounting, including most implementations of the UN System of Environmental-Economic Accounting — Ecosystem Accounting (SEEA EA, @UN2021seea), assigns ecosystem service values to the jurisdiction in which the ecosystem resides — not to the jurisdictions whose industries and consumers ultimately appropriate those services.

This misattribution matters for two reasons. First, it creates a fiscal externality: the costs of ecosystem maintenance are borne locally, while the benefits are distributed across the EU single market. Second, it biases the shadow prices used in cost-benefit analysis. Willingness-to-pay (WTP) estimates, the dominant method in the environmental economics literature [@sauer2012marginal], capture individual consumer preferences but not the systemic opportunity cost of ecosystem degradation for productive capacity. When a wetland is drained to expand agricultural output, the shadow price should reflect not just the welfare loss to local recreationists, but the full macroeconomic cost of losing the natural capital stock that supports transboundary supply chains.

We propose a methodological framework that computes **systemic shadow prices** for ecosystem services endogenously, by embedding a biophysical extension of the Leontief production system within a stock-flow consistent (SFC) macroeconomic model. The key insight is the Leontief price dual: if physical ecosystem service flows enter production as primary inputs alongside labor and capital, then the equilibrium price system must impute shadow values to those flows. We set these shadow values equal to the **marginal abatement cost (MAC)** — the expenditure required to restore degraded ecosystem assets to their SEEA EA baseline stock — which is the cost the economy would have to pay to maintain productive capacity indefinitely.

## Contribution

This paper makes three contributions to the literature.

**Methodological.** We provide the first operational integration of SEEA EA asset accounts, EE-MRIO trade matrices, and SFC macroeconomic dynamics in a unified shadow pricing framework. Prior work has either estimated ecosystem service values in partial equilibrium [@sauer2012marginal; @ehrlich2021contingent] or extended input-output models with environmental satellite accounts without closing the model through asset stock dynamics [@cazcarro2022figaroe3].

**Empirical.** Using the FIGARO-REG database [@cazcarro2022figaroe3; @garcia2025interregional], which disaggregates EU trade flows to 288 NUTS2 regions across 10 macro-sectors, combined with INCA ecosystem service use maps at 100m resolution, we compute the first transboundary shadow price vectors for EU ecosystem services at NUTS2 regional resolution. We demonstrate that shadow prices differ substantially across member states and that the trade-mediated component accounts for a significant share of total ecosystem valuation.

**Policy.** Our shadow price estimates have direct implications for the design of ecological fiscal transfers within the EU, the valuation of nature-based solutions in infrastructure appraisal, and the calibration of EU green accounting standards.

## Related Literature

Three strands of prior work are directly relevant.

**Environmentally extended input-output analysis.** @leontief1970environmental introduced the environmental repercussions table as an extension of the standard Leontief system, showing that pollution abatement requirements can be determined as part of the production price system. The subsequent MRIO literature extended this framework to global supply chains [@miller2009inputoutput] and has been used to compute carbon, water, and land footprints of international trade at high sectoral and regional resolution. Our contribution to this literature is a formal shadow pricing layer derived from the Leontief price *dual*: rather than treating ecosystem flows as physical satellite accounts, we solve for the equilibrium shadow price vector that would clear ecosystem service markets under a sustainability constraint. This endogenizes valuation within the IO system itself.

**Ecosystem service valuation.** The ES valuation literature has produced landmark aggregate estimates from meta-analyses of willingness-to-pay studies [@costanza1997value; @degroot2012global] and from partial-equilibrium cost assessments. These studies are foundational but share a common limitation: they are demand-side estimates reflecting consumer preferences at a point in time, without accounting for supply-chain amplification or asset stock dynamics. @sauer2012marginal take a step toward cost-based valuation by estimating marginal costs of joint agricultural and ecosystem outputs; we generalize this to a general equilibrium setting across all production sectors and link the cost measure explicitly to SEEA EA restoration accounts [@UN2021seea].

**Stock-flow consistent macroeconomics.** @godley2007monetary developed the SFC accounting framework as a rigorous tool for modeling the joint evolution of financial stocks and economic flows in an integrated balance sheet. Applied to environmental economics, SFC closures prevent the standard EE-MRIO inconsistency in which natural capital depletion shows up only in satellite accounts while leaving the production and financial system unchanged. Our model introduces ecosystem assets as a third wealth category — alongside produced capital and net financial claims — with their own stock dynamics and feedback to borrowing costs. This provides a fully consistent micro-to-macro chain from ecosystem intensity coefficients to shadow prices to interest rates to investment and final demand.

## Paper Structure

@sec-theory develops the theoretical framework. @sec-data describes the data sources and construction of empirical inputs. @sec-estimation presents the estimation strategy and shadow price solution. @sec-results reports main results and @sec-robustness presents robustness checks. @sec-welfare discusses welfare implications. @sec-conclusion concludes.

---

# Theoretical Framework {#sec-theory}

## Production Structure and Notation {#sec-prod}

Let the EU economy consist of $n$ regions (member states or NUTS2 areas) and $s$ sectors per region, giving $N = ns$ total production activities. Denote by $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^N_+$ the vector of gross outputs and by $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}_+$ the matrix of technical input coefficients, where $A_{ij}$ is the value of input from activity $i$ required per unit of output of activity $j$. Final demand is $\mathbf{F}(r) \in \mathbb{R}^N_+$, which depends on the macroeconomic interest rate $r$ through investment demand (the IS curve). The Leontief quantity system gives:

$$\mathbf{X} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{F}(r) \equiv \mathbf{L} \mathbf{F}(r)$$ {#eq-leontief}

where $\mathbf{L} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$ is the Leontief inverse.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Assumption 1 (Productive economy).** The matrix $\mathbf{A}$ satisfies the Hawkins-Simon conditions: all leading principal minors of $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$ are strictly positive. This ensures $\mathbf{L} \geq 0$ elementwise and that $\mathbf{X} \gg \mathbf{0}$ for any $\mathbf{F} \gg \mathbf{0}$.
:::

We augment the quantity system with a biophysical coefficient matrix $\mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{E \times N}_+$, where $E$ is the number of distinct ecosystem service flows. Element $R_{ej}$ denotes the physical volume of ecosystem service $e$ consumed per unit of gross output in activity $j$. Total ecosystem service demand is:

$$\mathbf{E}_{demand} = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{L} \mathbf{F}(r)$$ {#eq-es-demand}

The matrix $\mathbf{M} \equiv \mathbf{R}\mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{R}^{E \times N}_+$ is the **ecosystem service multiplier matrix**: $M_{ej}$ gives the total ecosystem service requirement — direct and supply-chain embedded — per unit of final demand in activity $j$.

## Ecosystem Asset Stocks and the Sustainability Constraint {#sec-assets}

Following SEEA EA conventions [@UN2021seea], each ecosystem service flow $e$ is drawn from an underlying asset stock $K_e(t) \geq 0$, measured in physical units. Stock-flow consistency requires:

$$\dot{K}_e(t) = G_e(K_e) - E_{demand,e}(t) + \mu_e(t)$$ {#eq-sfc}

where $G_e : \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}_+$ is the natural regeneration function and $\mu_e(t) \geq 0$ is active restoration effort (abatement). We assume $G_e$ is concave and attains its maximum sustainable yield at $K_e^*$. We define the **SEEA baseline stock** $\bar{K}_e$ as the opening physical stock in the reference period. The sustainability constraint requires asset stocks to remain non-declining:

$$\dot{K}_e(t) \geq 0 \iff E_{demand,e}(t) \leq G_e(K_e) + \mu_e(t) \quad \forall e$$ {#eq-sustainability}

## The Market Failure {#sec-failure}

In the unregulated market equilibrium, ecosystem services are unpriced. Firms choose output $\mathbf{X}$ to satisfy final demand at given prices, treating $\mathbf{R}$ as a free input. The resulting ecosystem service demand $\mathbf{E}^{mkt} = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F}(r_0)$ need not satisfy @eq-sustainability. The market failure is twofold:

**Open-access externality.** No single firm bears the full cost of ecosystem stock depletion because ecosystem services are rival in consumption (drawn from a finite stock) but non-excludable in production — a classic open-access commons problem [@hardin1968tragedy].

**Transboundary externality.** Ecosystem assets are geographically fixed. When production in region $j$ draws on ecosystem services located in region $k \neq j$ via supply-chain linkages (captured by $L_{kj} > 0$), the external cost of depletion in $k$ accrues to firms in $j$ without any market mechanism to internalize it. This generates a fiscal externality across member states: the costs of ecosystem maintenance are borne locally in $k$, while productive gains flow to $j$.

## The Social Planner's Problem and Shadow Prices {#sec-planner}

A benevolent social planner chooses final demand $\mathbf{F}$ and restoration effort $\boldsymbol{\mu} \geq \mathbf{0}$ to maximize aggregate welfare $\mathcal{W}(\mathbf{F})$ — a strictly increasing, concave function of final demand — subject to production feasibility, sustainability, and a budget constraint on restoration expenditure.

Let $c_e(\mu_e)$ denote the (convex, increasing) cost of restoration effort $\mu_e$ for ecosystem $e$. The planner's static problem is:

$$\max_{\mathbf{F},\, \boldsymbol{\mu} \geq \mathbf{0}} \quad \mathcal{W}(\mathbf{F}) - \boldsymbol{\gamma}^\top \mathbf{c}(\boldsymbol{\mu})$$ {#eq-planner}

subject to:

$$\mathbf{R} \mathbf{L} \mathbf{F} \leq \mathbf{G}(\bar{\mathbf{K}}) + \boldsymbol{\mu} \qquad [\boldsymbol{\lambda}]$$ {#eq-sust-constraint}

$$\mathbf{F} \geq \mathbf{0}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu} \geq \mathbf{0}$$ {#eq-nonnegativity}

where $\mathbf{G}(\bar{\mathbf{K}}) = (G_e(\bar{K}_e))_{e=1}^E$ is the vector of maximum sustainable yields at the baseline stock, $\gamma > 0$ is the welfare weight on the numeraire, and $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \in \mathbb{R}^E_+$ is the vector of Lagrange multipliers on the sustainability constraints.

The Lagrangian is $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{W}(\mathbf{F}) - \boldsymbol{\gamma}^\top \mathbf{c}(\boldsymbol{\mu}) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^\top \left[\mathbf{G}(\bar{\mathbf{K}}) + \boldsymbol{\mu} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F}\right]$.

**First-order conditions.** For an interior solution in $\mathbf{F}$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{F}} \mathcal{W}(\mathbf{F}^*) = \mathbf{L}^\top \mathbf{R}^\top \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*$$ {#eq-foc-demand}

The left-hand side is the marginal welfare value of final demand; the right-hand side is the shadow cost of the ecosystem services required to produce it, compounded through all upstream supply-chain linkages. For restoration, the complementary slackness conditions give:

$$\mu_e^* > 0 \implies \lambda_e^* = \gamma \cdot c_e'(\mu_e^*) \equiv \gamma \cdot \text{MAC}_e$$ {#eq-foc-restoration}

The multiplier $\lambda_e^*$ equals the marginal abatement cost when restoration is actively deployed. The planner's shadow price on ecosystem service $e$ is therefore $\lambda_e^* / \gamma = \text{MAC}_e$.

## Shadow Price Identification and the Leontief Price Dual {#sec-dual}

The Leontief price dual characterizes the full cost structure of production. If each ecosystem service carries shadow price $P_{shadow,e} = \lambda_e^* / \gamma$, producer prices must satisfy:

$$\mathbf{P}^\top = \mathbf{P}^\top \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{V}^\top + \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R}$$ {#eq-price-dual}

where $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^N_+$ is the vector of conventional value added (wages, profits, taxes net of subsidies). Solving:

$$\mathbf{P}^\top = (\mathbf{V}^\top + \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R})\,\mathbf{L}$$ {#eq-prices-solution}

This system has a unique solution under Assumption 1 and yields producer prices that are strictly positive whenever $\mathbf{V} \gg \mathbf{0}$ or $\mathbf{P}_{shadow} \gg \mathbf{0}$.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
**Proposition 1 (MAC identification).** *Suppose restoration technology has constant unit costs: $c_e(\mu_e) = \overline{c}_e \mu_e$ for all $e$. Then at the social optimum, the shadow price vector satisfies $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}^* = (\overline{c}_1, \ldots, \overline{c}_E)^\top \equiv \mathbf{\overline{c}}$, i.e., the vector of marginal abatement costs. If restoration costs are strictly convex ($c_e'' > 0$), then $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}^* \geq \mathbf{\overline{c}}$ with equality only when abatement fully resolves the sustainability deficit.*

*Proof.* Under linear costs, the FOC @eq-foc-restoration gives $\lambda_e^* = \gamma \overline{c}_e$ for any $\mu_e^* > 0$. Dividing by $\gamma$ yields the result. Under convex costs, $c_e'(\mu_e^*)$ is increasing in $\mu_e^*$; since a binding sustainability constraint requires positive restoration, $\lambda_e^* / \gamma = c_e'(\mu_e^*) \geq c_e'(0) \equiv \overline{c}_e$. $\square$
:::

Under Proposition 1, empirically observable restoration costs (EU LIFE programme, national remediation accounts) directly identify $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}$, without requiring estimates of welfare weights or preference parameters. This shifts valuation from the demand side (WTP surveys) to the supply side (restoration cost engineering), aligning with the SEEA EA framework's cost-based valuation philosophy.

## Transboundary Shadow Cost Pass-Through {#sec-passthrough}

The term $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R} \mathbf{L}$ in @eq-prices-solution is the vector of **supply-chain-embedded ecosystem costs** per unit of final demand. Let $\boldsymbol{\tau} \equiv \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R} \mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times N}$ denote this row vector. The $j$-th element $\tau_j$ is the full ecosystem shadow cost — direct plus upstream — of one unit of final demand in activity $j$.

To decompose domestic from foreign components, partition the activity index as $j = (k, \sigma)$ where $k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$ is the destination region and $\sigma \in \{1, \ldots, s\}$ is the sector. Similarly, write $\mathbf{R} = [\mathbf{R}^{(k)}]_{k}$ where $\mathbf{R}^{(k)}$ collects the columns of $\mathbf{R}$ belonging to region $k$. Then:

$$\tau_{k\sigma} = \underbrace{\mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R}^{(k)}_{*\sigma}}_{\text{direct domestic}} + \underbrace{\sum_{k' \neq k} \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R}^{(k')} \mathbf{L}^{(k'k)}_{*\sigma}}_{\text{transboundary upstream}}$$ {#eq-decomp}

where $\mathbf{L}^{(k'k)}$ is the sub-block of the Leontief inverse giving the output requirements in region $k'$ per unit of final demand in region $k$.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
**Proposition 2 (Transboundary amplification).** *Let $\mathbf{D} \equiv \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R}$ be the vector of direct ecosystem costs per unit of gross output. Then $\tau_j \geq D_j$ for all $j$, with strict inequality whenever $\sum_{i \neq j} D_i L_{ij} > 0$. That is, the supply-chain-embedded ecosystem cost weakly exceeds the direct cost, with strict amplification whenever upstream activities in other sectors or regions carry positive direct ecosystem costs.*

*Proof.* By definition, $\tau_j = \sum_{i=1}^N D_i L_{ij}$. Since $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{I} + \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^2 + \cdots$ (Neumann series), $L_{jj} \geq 1$ and $L_{ij} \geq 0$ for all $i,j$ under Assumption 1. Therefore $\tau_j = D_j L_{jj} + \sum_{i \neq j} D_i L_{ij} \geq D_j$. Strict inequality holds iff $\sum_{i \neq j} D_i L_{ij} > 0$. $\square$
:::

Proposition 2 formalizes the intuition that single-region or direct-attribution approaches *systematically understate* the true ecosystem cost of production. The amplification factor for activity $j$ is $\tau_j / D_j = \sum_i D_i L_{ij} / D_j$, which depends on the upstream input structure and the spatial distribution of ecosystem intensities.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Corollary 1 (Ecosystem Border Adjustment).** *Suppose each region $k$ levies a domestic ecosystem tax equal to the direct cost per unit of output: $\mathbf{t}_k = \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R}^{(k)}$, applied uniformly to all production activities $\sigma \in \{1,\ldots,s\}$ in that region. Define the upstream embedded ecosystem cost of imports of good $\sigma$ from region $k'$ to region $k$ as:*

$$\hat{t}_{k'k\sigma} \;=\; \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R}^{(k')} \mathbf{L}^{(k'k)}_{*\sigma}$$

*Then a border adjustment tariff $\hat{t}_{k'k\sigma} - t_{k'} \cdot \mathbf{1}_{k' \text{ taxes}}$ levied at the border of $k$ on imports from $k'$ — equal to the upstream ecosystem cost embedded in those imports minus any domestic ecosystem tax already paid by producers in $k'$ — decentralizes the social optimum. In the absence of any domestic ecosystem taxes ($\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{0}$), the optimal tariff rate reduces to $\hat{t}_{k'k\sigma} = \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R}^{(k')} \mathbf{L}^{(k'k)}_{*\sigma}$, which corresponds to a generalization of the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) to all ecosystem service flows.*
:::

Corollary 1 establishes that correct ecosystem pricing requires coordination across member states: a jurisdiction that prices ecosystem services domestically without a border adjustment creates a competitive disadvantage relative to importers from unregulated regions (*carbon leakage* generalized to ecosystem leakage). Conversely, a unilateral border adjustment without domestic pricing is insufficient. The socially optimal instrument set requires both simultaneously — an insight with direct implications for EU ecological fiscal architecture.

## The SFC–IS–LM Closure and Asset Dynamics {#sec-macro}

Propositions 1 and 2 characterize shadow prices in a static setting. We embed them in a dynamic macro model by closing the system through asset stock dynamics and financial market feedbacks.

**Asset dynamics.** Combining @eq-sfc and the sustainability constraint @eq-sust-constraint, the stock evolution under the social optimum is:

$$\dot{K}_e = G_e(K_e) - (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F})_e + \mu_e^* \geq 0$$ {#eq-stock-dynamics}

At the sustainable steady state, $\dot{K}_e = 0$ and $E_{demand,e} = G_e(\bar{K}_e) + \mu_e^*$: demand equals regeneration plus restoration. Departures from this steady state — caused by demand shocks, technological change, or policy gaps — propagate via @eq-stock-dynamics and alter the binding MAC values and hence $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}$.

**The LM channel.** Following @godley2007monetary and the natural capital balance sheet literature, we model ecosystem assets as a component of the national wealth account. Let $\Omega_k(t) = \sum_e P_{shadow,e} K_e^{(k)}(t)$ be the natural capital value of region $k$'s asset stocks at current shadow prices. Asset depletion reduces $\Omega_k$, impairs the balance sheet of sovereign and bank borrowers in region $k$, and raises the risk premium on long-term capital. The aggregate interest rate across EU regions satisfies:

$$r(t) = r_0 + \phi \cdot \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^n \max\!\left(0,\, \frac{\bar{\Omega}_k - \Omega_k(t)}{\bar{\Omega}_k}\right)$$ {#eq-interest-rate}

where $\phi > 0$ is the ecological risk premium parameter and $\bar{\Omega}_k$ is the baseline natural capital value. This specification is parsimonious but captures the essential feedback: degradation in any member state raises the common borrowing rate (reflecting EU financial market integration), which dampens investment demand $\mathbf{F}(r)$ through the IS curve, which in turn reduces total ecosystem demand $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F}(r)$.

**The IS–LM–Ecosystem equilibrium.** A full short-run equilibrium is a triple $(\mathbf{X}^*, \mathbf{P}^*, r^*)$ satisfying simultaneously:

$$\mathbf{X}^* = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbf{F}(r^*) \tag{IS}$$
$$r^* = r_0 + \phi \cdot \Delta\Omega(\mathbf{K}) \tag{LM}$$
$$\mathbf{P}^{*\top} = (\mathbf{V}^\top + \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R})\,\mathbf{L} \tag{Price dual}$$

The system is block-recursive: given asset stocks $\mathbf{K}$ (predetermined at any date $t$), the LM equation pins down $r^*$, the IS equation gives $\mathbf{X}^*$, and the price dual gives $\mathbf{P}^*$. Over time, $\mathbf{K}$ evolves via @eq-stock-dynamics, shifting $r^*$ and feeding back into $\mathbf{X}^*$. The model therefore generates an endogenous path of shadow prices that responds to the trajectory of ecosystem degradation.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
**Proposition 3 (Ecological ratchet).** *Suppose restoration costs are increasing in the cumulative deficit:*

$$\overline{c}_e(t) = \overline{c}_e^0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\delta_e \int_0^t \max\!\left(0,\, E_{demand,e}(s) - G_e(K_e(s))\right)ds\right), \quad \delta_e > 0$$

*Then along any market-equilibrium trajectory in which the sustainability constraint is violated at some interval $[t_0, t_1]$: (i) the marginal abatement cost $\overline{c}_e(t)$ is strictly increasing on $[t_0, t_1]$; (ii) the social optimum shadow price $P_{shadow,e}^*(t) = \overline{c}_e(t)$ is non-decreasing; and (iii) the pass-through cost $\tau_j(t)$ is strictly increasing on $[t_0, t_1]$ for any activity $j$ with $D_j > 0$. Uncorrected market equilibria therefore generate self-reinforcing under-valuation: delayed pricing leads to higher future shadow prices.*

*Proof.* Define the cumulative deficit $\Phi_e(t) = \int_0^t \max(0, E_{demand,e}(s) - G_e(K_e(s)))\,ds$. On $[t_0, t_1]$, $E_{demand,e}(s) > G_e(K_e(s))$, so $\dot{\Phi}_e(t) > 0$: the deficit grows strictly. Since $\overline{c}_e(t) = \overline{c}_e^0 e^{\delta_e \Phi_e(t)}$, we have $\frac{d}{dt}\overline{c}_e(t) = \delta_e \overline{c}_e(t) \dot{\Phi}_e(t) > 0$, establishing (i). Under Proposition 1, $P_{shadow,e}^*(t) = \overline{c}_e(t)$, so (ii) follows. For (iii), $\tau_j(t) = \mathbf{P}_{shadow}(t)^\top \mathbf{R} \mathbf{L}_j$; since $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{L} \geq 0$ (Assumption 1) and at least one $R_{ej} > 0$ with $P_{shadow,e}(t)$ strictly increasing for $D_j > 0$, $\tau_j(t)$ is strictly increasing. $\square$
:::

Proposition 3 formalizes a key policy implication: the longer ecosystem service pricing is delayed, the higher the eventual shadow price and the larger the wealth transfer from future to present generations implicit in current consumption patterns. The proposition also implies that historical nc-NDP statistics (if they had been computed) would have understated the overstatement of welfare — a form of compounding valuation bias.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
**Proposition 4 (Short-run equilibrium existence and uniqueness).** *Suppose (i) $\mathbf{F}: \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}^N_+$ is continuous and strictly componentwise decreasing in $r$, with $\lim_{r\to\infty} F_j(r) = 0$; (ii) $\phi \geq 0$; and (iii) Assumption 1 holds. Then for any given stock vector $\mathbf{K} \gg \mathbf{0}$ and predetermined $\mathbf{P}_{shadow} \geq \mathbf{0}$, there exists a unique short-run equilibrium triple $(r^*, \mathbf{X}^*, \mathbf{P}^*)$.*

*Proof.* The LM equation determines $r^* = r_0 + \phi \cdot \Delta\Omega(\mathbf{K})$ uniquely from $\mathbf{K}$ (a finite sum of bounded terms). Given $r^*$, $\mathbf{F}(r^*)$ is uniquely determined by (i). Under Assumption 1, $\mathbf{L}$ exists, is non-negative, and yields $\mathbf{X}^* = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{F}(r^*) \gg \mathbf{0}$. Finally, $\mathbf{P}^{*\top} = (\mathbf{V}^\top + \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R})\mathbf{L}$ is uniquely determined under Assumption 1. $\square$
:::

Proposition 4 confirms that the IS–LM–Ecosystem system is well-defined and block-recursive: given the state of natural capital, the short-run equilibrium reduces to a sequence of linear solves rather than a fixed-point problem. This tractability is the key to computational feasibility at NUTS2 scale.

---

# Data {#sec-data}

## Economic Backbone: FIGARO-REG

The structural backbone of the model is provided by the **Full International and Global Accounts for Research in Input-Output Analysis — Regional** (FIGARO-REG) tables, specifically the 10-sector inter-regional IO matrix (`IOI_10SEC_D2`) for 2017 [@cazcarro2022figaroe3; @garcia2025interregional], produced by the JRC. FIGARO-REG disaggregates EU economic activity into 10 NACE macro-sectors — agriculture and fishing (A), mining and utilities (B–E), construction (F), trade/transport/hospitality (G–I), ICT (J), finance (K), real estate (L), professional services (M–N), public services (O–Q), and arts and recreation (R–U) — across 288 NUTS2 regions in 29 countries, with bilateral inter-regional trade flows estimated using gravity-model-based allocation of Eurostat's national accounts.

The raw file is a flat record table of 24.5 million rows, each recording a bilateral flow REFREG × ROWII → COUNTERPARTREG × COLII in millions of euros. We parse this into a sparse intermediate flow matrix $\mathbf{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ (where $N = 10 \times n_{NUTS2}$), a final demand matrix $\mathbf{F}$, and a value-added vector $\mathbf{v}$, using Julia's sparse matrix toolkit. Gross output is $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{1} + \mathbf{F}\mathbf{1}$, and the technical coefficient matrix is $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{Z}\,\text{diag}(\mathbf{x})^{-1}$. The Leontief inverse is never formed explicitly; linear system solves $(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$ are handled by Julia's sparse direct solver.

## Ecosystem Service Flow Accounts (INCA)

Ecosystem service flow volumes are sourced from the **Integrated Natural Capital Accounting (INCA)** platform, produced by the JRC. INCA provides harmonized Supply and Use tables for ecosystem services at national level, aligned with SEEA EA accounting conventions, covering EU-27 member states for the years 2000, 2006, 2012, and 2018 in both physical and monetary units. We use the 2018 physical use tables to match the FIGARO-REG base year as closely as possible. The INCA accounts cover nine ecosystem service types:

| INCA ES | Physical unit | FIGARO sector link |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| Global climate regulation | kt CO₂-eq | All (universal sink) |
| Crop pollination | tonnes crop yield | Agriculture (A) |
| Wood provision | m³ timber | Agriculture/forestry (A) |
| Flood control | hectares protected | All (public good) |
| Air filtration | tonnes PM removed | Industry (B–E, F) |
| Soil retention | tonnes sediment | Agriculture (A) |
| Crop provision | tonnes biomass | Agriculture (A) |
| Nature-based tourism | visitor-days | Services (G–I, R–U) |
| Habitat and species maintenance | million € | All sectors (universal) |

**NUTS2 disaggregation.** National-level INCA use totals are disaggregated to NUTS2 regions using spatial shares derived from INCA's high-resolution use maps: 100m resolution GeoTIFFs in EPSG:3035 (ETRS89/LAEA Europe), covering the 2018 reference year. For each ecosystem service, we compute zonal sums over each NUTS2 polygon (reprojected from EPSG:4326 to EPSG:3035) using windowed raster reads via the Python `rasterio` library. The NUTS2 share for region $k$ in country $c$ is $\omega_{ek} = \text{spatial\_sum}_{ek} / \sum_{k' \in c} \text{spatial\_sum}_{ek'}$, and the regional use intensity is $R_{ej} = \omega_{ek} \cdot \text{INCA\_national}_{ec} / x_j$. NUTS2 boundaries are sourced from Eurostat GISCO (2021 vintage, LEVL\_2, EPSG:4326), covering 334 EU NUTS2 polygons in total. Spatial shares are computed for all 334 regions and then subset to the 288 regions present in FIGARO-REG; the remainder (mostly island territories and overseas regions not covered by FIGARO) are excluded from the model.

## Ecosystem Asset Accounts (SEEA EA)

Ecosystem extent data are sourced from the **Copernicus Land Monitoring Service (CLMS)**, specifically **CORINE Land Cover (CLC)** at 100m resolution, crosswalked to the EU Ecosystem Typology (EUNIS Level 2). Condition data are assembled from:

- *Physical/chemical:* LUCAS topsoil parameters (JRC) and EEA water quality indices
- *Biological/structural:* Common Bird and Butterfly Indices (EBCC) and satellite-derived Leaf Area Index (LAI) from Copernicus

## Marginal Abatement Costs

Shadow prices are anchored to ecosystem restoration cost estimates from the EU LIFE programme database, national habitat remediation cost assessments, and the literature on nature-based solution costs [@sauer2012marginal]. For the baseline empirical implementation we use constant unit costs $\overline{c}_e$ per unit of ecosystem deficit (Proposition 1, linear case); sensitivity analysis under convex costs is reported in @sec-robustness.

---

# Estimation {#sec-estimation}

## Overview and Identification

The empirical implementation proceeds in four stages that map directly to the theoretical system in @sec-theory.

**Stage 1: Constructing $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{L}$.** From the FIGARO-REG flat file, we construct the sparse intermediate flow matrix $\mathbf{Z}$, gross output vector $\mathbf{x}$, and technical coefficient matrix $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{Z}\,\text{diag}(\mathbf{x})^{-1}$. We verify Assumption 1 (Hawkins-Simon) numerically by confirming that all eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ have modulus less than one, so that the Neumann series $\sum_{k=0}^\infty \mathbf{A}^k$ converges. The Leontief inverse is never stored explicitly; products $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{v}$ are computed by solving $(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{v}$ using Julia's sparse LU factorization.

**Stage 2: Constructing $\mathbf{R}$.** For each ecosystem service $e$ and each NUTS2 region $k$, we allocate the national INCA physical use flow $U_{ec}$ (from the 2018 use tables, country $c(k)$) to individual NUTS2 regions using the spatial shares $\omega_{ek}$ derived from the zonal statistics pipeline (Script 07). Within each NUTS2 region, we further allocate total ES use across the $s$ FIGARO sectors using the INCA sector taxonomy: agriculture-linked ES (crop pollination, soil retention, crop provision, wood provision) are attributed entirely to sector A; industry-linked ES (air filtration) to sectors B–E and F proportional to value added; and universal ES (global climate regulation, flood control) are attributed to all sectors proportional to gross output. The resulting intensity coefficient is:

$$R_{e,ks} = \frac{\omega_{ek} \cdot U_{e,c(k)} \cdot \alpha_{es}}{x_{ks}}$$

where $\alpha_{es}$ is the sector allocation weight (summing to one over $s$ for each $e$) and $x_{ks}$ is gross output of NUTS2 region $k$, sector $s$.

**Stage 3: Shadow price identification.** Under Proposition 1 with linear restoration costs, shadow prices are directly set to $P_{shadow,e} = \overline{c}_e$, the marginal abatement cost for ecosystem service $e$. In the baseline specification, we use restoration cost benchmarks from the literature and EU LIFE programme expenditure data, scaled to the physical units of each INCA ES. The main sensitivity dimension is the specification of $\overline{c}_e$ (cost per unit of ES deficit); @sec-robustness reports the full sensitivity analysis.

**Stage 4: Shadow cost pass-through.** Given $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}$, $\mathbf{R}$, and $\mathbf{L}$, we compute:

- The direct ecosystem cost vector: $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times N}$
- The total pass-through vector: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R} \mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times N}$
- The transboundary amplification ratio for each activity: $\rho_j = \tau_j / D_j$
- The domestic share: $\delta_{kj} = D_{kj} L_{kj} / \tau_j$ (share of total shadow cost attributable to local ecosystem use)

The decomposition in @eq-decomp is computed by multiplying out the block-partitioned $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{L}$ matrices — leveraging the sparsity structure of the FIGARO-REG inter-regional flows, which are zero for many region-pair combinations.

## Computation

All matrix operations are implemented in Julia using `SparseArrays.jl`. The sparse LU factorization of $(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})$ is performed once; subsequent solves for different right-hand sides (different columns of $\mathbf{R}$ and different shadow price vectors) are cheap triangular substitutions. Total runtime for the full $N = 2{,}880$-dimensional system is approximately 30 seconds on a standard laptop.

## Marginal Abatement Cost Calibration

We calibrate $\overline{c}_e$ from three sources:

1. **EU LIFE programme**: Average cost per hectare restored, per unit of carbon sequestered, or per unit of flood protection across funded restoration projects (2014–2020 programming period).
2. **Habitat remediation assessments**: National biodiversity offset registry data for Germany, France, and Sweden (with extrapolation to other member states using per-capita GDP adjustment).
3. **Literature benchmarks**: Meta-analytic estimates from @sauer2012marginal and related cost-function literature.

Where multiple sources are available, we take the geometric mean as the baseline and use the interquartile range across sources to define the robustness interval in @sec-robustness.

---

# Results {#sec-results}

*Note: The following results use NUTS2 spatial disaggregation for seven ecosystem services (global climate regulation, crop pollination, wood provision, flood control, soil retention, crop provision, nature-based recreation) and national-level SUT allocation for two (water purification, habitat and species maintenance). Spatial disaggregation for air filtration is pending completion of a large data download; once available, results will be updated. The country-level patterns and amplification structure are not expected to change materially.*

## Shadow Price Magnitudes and Verification

Table 1 reports the shadow price vector $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}^*$ calibrated to restoration cost benchmarks. Proposition 1 is implemented in the linear cost case: $P_{shadow,e}^* = \overline{c}_e$, the marginal abatement cost per physical unit of ecosystem service deficit.

| Ecosystem Service | Physical Unit | MAC ($\overline{c}_e$) | NUTS2 regions with spatial data |
|-------------------|--------------|------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Carbon sequestration | kt CO₂ | 0.085 million€/kt | 249/334 |
| Crop pollination | kt crop yield | 0.15 million€/kt | 240/334 |
| Crop provision | kt dry matter | 0.12 million€/kt | 0/334 (SUT national only) |
| Wood provision | k m³ | 0.045 million€/km³ | 233/334 |
| Flood control | million€ avoided damage | 1.0 million€/million€ | 223/334 |
| Soil retention | kt soil | 0.008 million€/kt | 0/334 (SUT national only) |
| Water purification | kt N removed | 12.0 million€/kt | 0/334 (SUT national only) |
| Nature-based recreation | k visitor-days | 0.05 million€/k visits | 251/334 |
| Habitat and species | million€ | 1.0 million€/million€ | 0/334 (SUT national only) |

**Verification of Proposition 2.** Across all $N = 2{,}880$ NUTS2 × sector activities, the transboundary amplification inequality $\tau_j \geq D_j$ holds with zero violations. The mean amplification ratio $\tau_j / D_j$ is approximately 1,049 across activities with positive direct costs. The high ratio reflects two compounding effects: first, the standard Leontief multiplier (supply-chain pass-through accumulates ecosystem costs over all upstream tiers); and second, spatial concentration — many NUTS2 regions have zero direct ecosystem intensity $D_j$ for a given ES, so their small but positive $\tau_j$ (from upstream imports) generates an extreme ratio. The ratio is highest for service sectors (ICT, finance, professional services) in regions with no local primary production.

## Total Ecosystem Shadow Costs

The total supply-chain-embedded ecosystem shadow cost of EU final demand, computed as $\sum_j \tau_j F_j$, is approximately **€1.83 trillion** for 2017. This represents about 12% of EU GDP (2017 EU-27+UK GDP: approximately €15.3 trillion) — the share of economic value at stake if ecosystem services were priced at their restoration cost. As a comparison benchmark, switching to a uniform within-country NUTS2 allocation (ignoring spatial heterogeneity) yields €1.91 trillion, a 4% difference in aggregate — confirming that the level is robust to the spatial disaggregation method, while the regional distribution changes substantially.

Of the total €1.83 trillion, approximately **€50 billion** (2.7%) represents transboundary shadow costs — ecosystem restoration obligations that cross national borders through supply-chain linkages. This is the lower bound on the fiscal externality that ecological fiscal transfer mechanisms should address. The figure rises as additional ES spatial maps (air filtration, soil retention for full NUTS2 coverage) are incorporated; the ES types pending spatial data have lower MACs, so the impact on the total is modest.

## Cross-Country Heterogeneity

@tbl-country reports the top countries ranked by demand-weighted shadow cost $\tau$ (final-demand-weighted average transboundary shadow cost rate).

| Country | $\bar{\tau}$ (demand-weighted) | Foreign share | Interpretation |
|---------|-------------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Latvia (LV) | 0.292 | 3.0% | Forestry-intensive, high domestic ecosystem use |
| Lithuania (LT) | 0.261 | 5.6% | Agriculture and forestry dominant |
| Bulgaria (BG) | 0.242 | 3.7% | Agriculture with low-input yields |
| Poland (PL) | 0.233 | 6.6% | Large agricultural economy, integrated supply chains |
| Finland (FI) | 0.194 | 3.4% | Forestry sector dominance |

: Demand-weighted average shadow cost rate by country (top 5). {#tbl-country}

The shadow cost rate $\bar{\tau}$ is the average ecosystem shadow cost per million euro of final demand, in units of million€/million€ (dimensionless). Latvia's rate of 0.292 means that for every €1 million of Latvian final demand, approximately €292,000 of ecosystem restoration cost is embedded in the supply chain. The low foreign shares for Latvia, Lithuania, and Finland reflect that these economies' ecosystem footprints are primarily domestic — their forests and agricultural land are used directly by their own industries rather than imported from abroad. Poland's higher foreign share (6.6%) reflects its deeper integration with EU supply chains as a large manufacturing hub.

Countries at the bottom of the distribution (not shown) are typically service-intensive economies with lower direct ecosystem use and lower amplification because their upstream supply chains source fewer primary inputs domestically.

## Amplification and the Leontief Multiplier

The mean amplification ratio of approximately 1,049 across activities with positive direct costs reflects two compounding mechanisms. First, the standard Leontief multiplier effect: each round of production requires inputs from suppliers, who require their own inputs, so direct ecosystem costs compound through all tiers. Second, spatial concentration: with NUTS2-level spatial shares, many regions have $D_j = 0$ for a given ES (they are not in the primary production zone for that service), yet receive a positive $\tau_j$ through imports from ES-intensive regions elsewhere. The result is high amplification ratios for service-sector regions that source ecosystem-intensive inputs from agricultural and forestry regions in other NUTS2 areas. This spatial dimension is precisely the mechanism that Proposition 2 formalizes: ignoring upstream supply-chain linkages and cross-regional sourcing systematically understates ecosystem costs, and the understatement is largest for sectors and regions most distant from primary production.

---

# Robustness {#sec-robustness}

We assess robustness along four dimensions.

**MAC calibration uncertainty.** The baseline shadow price vector $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}$ is calibrated to central estimates of marginal abatement costs. We report results for the full interquartile range of MAC estimates from the EU LIFE programme and remediation literature, scaling each $\overline{c}_e$ by factors of $\{0.5, 1.0, 2.0\}$ independently. Since the price dual @eq-prices-solution is linear in $\mathbf{P}_{shadow}$, the resulting pass-through vector $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ scales proportionally; robustness here reduces to uncertainty in the overall level of shadow prices, not their relative cross-regional pattern.

**Sector allocation weights.** Our baseline assigns ES use within NUTS2 regions to FIGARO sectors using a fixed allocation rule $\alpha_{es}$ (agriculture-attributed ES go entirely to sector A, etc.). We test sensitivity to: (i) proportional allocation of all ES to sectors by value-added share; (ii) alternative crosswalk schemes based on INCA's own sector taxonomy. Preliminary analysis suggests the transboundary amplification ratio $\tau_j / D_j$ is robust to sector allocation, since it is driven primarily by the Leontief multipliers rather than the distribution of direct intensities.

**NUTS2 spatial shares.** The baseline NUTS2 disaggregation uses spatial shares $\omega_{ek}$ from 100m GeoTIFF zonal statistics, available for seven ES types. For the remaining two (water purification, habitat and species maintenance), we fall back to uniform within-country allocation. We can directly compare the spatial vs. uniform approaches: switching to uniform within-country allocation for all ES yields an aggregate shadow cost of €1.91 trillion, versus €1.83 trillion in the baseline — a 4% difference in the aggregate level. The regional distribution changes substantially, however: under uniform allocation, all NUTS2 regions within a country receive identical shadow cost rates, while the spatial baseline concentrates ecosystem costs in production-intensive regions. We additionally compare to population-weighted and land-use-weighted (CORINE Land Cover) allocation as sensitivity checks. Spatial shares matter most for ES that are geographically concentrated (crop pollination follows agricultural land distribution; wood provision follows forested area), and least for universal ES (climate regulation, where even the 100m map shows near-uniform coverage).

**Base year.** FIGARO-REG is available only for 2017. INCA provides use tables for 2000, 2006, 2012, and 2018. The 2018 INCA data is used in the baseline to minimize the base-year gap. We also report results using 2012 INCA data to assess the stability of regional patterns across time.

---

# Welfare Implications {#sec-welfare}

## Measuring the Ecological Valuation Gap

Define the **ecological valuation gap** for member state $k$ as the difference between the true systemic shadow cost of ecosystem services embedded in its final demand and the shadow cost that would be computed using only direct, domestic ES intensities:

$$\text{Gap}_k = \sum_\sigma \left(\tau_{k\sigma} - D_{k\sigma}\right) F_{k\sigma} = \sum_\sigma \left(\sum_{k' \neq k} \mathbf{P}_{shadow}^\top \mathbf{R}^{(k')} \mathbf{L}^{(k'k)}_{*\sigma}\right) F_{k\sigma}$$

By Proposition 2, $\text{Gap}_k \geq 0$ for all $k$. The gap is strictly positive for member states that are net importers of ecosystem-intensive goods from other EU regions. $\text{Gap}_k$ measures the magnitude of the **fiscal externality** borne by ecosystem-rich member states on behalf of ecosystem-intensive importers — the amount of ecosystem maintenance cost externalized across borders without compensating transfers.

## Ecological Fiscal Transfers and Green Budget Reform

A direct policy application of our shadow price estimates is the calibration of **ecological fiscal transfers (EFTs)** within the EU. Under an optimal EFT scheme, member state $k'$ (the ecosystem-providing region) would receive a transfer from member state $k$ (the ecosystem-importing region) equal to $\hat{t}_{k'k\sigma} \cdot F_{k\sigma}$ — the shadow cost of the upstream ecosystem use in $k'$ attributable to $k$'s final demand. Summing over all trading partners:

$$\text{EFT}_{k'} = \sum_{k \neq k'} \sum_\sigma \hat{t}_{k'k\sigma} F_{k\sigma}$$

This is the net transfer that would compensate $k'$ for the ecosystem maintenance costs externalized to it through the EU single market. The total EU-wide transfer pool is $\sum_{k'} \text{EFT}_{k'} = \boldsymbol{\tau}'\mathbf{F} - \mathbf{D}'\mathbf{F}$ (total transboundary shadow cost of final demand), which our preliminary estimates put at approximately €50 billion per year (to be updated with full spatial data).

This framework connects directly to ongoing EU policy discussions around the **Biodiversity Net Gain** regulation, the **Nature Restoration Law** financing gap, and proposed extensions of the EU Emissions Trading System to land use and forestry. Our shadow prices provide a principled, market-consistent alternative to the ad hoc biophysical weighting schemes currently used in most EFT proposals.

## Natural Capital Adjusted Net Domestic Product

A second welfare application is the construction of **natural capital adjusted NDP** (nc-NDP). Standard NDP deducts depreciation of produced capital from GDP. We propose an additional deduction for ecosystem asset depletion valued at shadow prices:

$$\text{nc-NDP}_k = \text{NDP}_k - \sum_e P_{shadow,e} \cdot \max\!\left(0,\, E_{demand,e}^{(k)} - G_e(K_e^{(k)})\right)$$

The deduction is the shadow cost of the sustainability deficit: the amount of ecosystem degradation not compensated by natural regeneration, valued at the marginal restoration cost. Unlike defensive expenditure adjustments (which subtract only actual remediation spending), nc-NDP adjusts for the *implicit* cost of deferred restoration — the amount the economy would need to spend in the future to maintain non-declining natural capital, valued in present-day prices.

By Proposition 3 (ecological ratchet), delays in pricing lead to higher future shadow prices, which retroactively reveal that nc-NDP was understated in earlier periods. Our framework therefore suggests that official GDP statistics have been *systematically overstating* economic welfare in EU member states with significant ecosystem deficits — and that the overstatement compounds over time.

## Distributional Analysis

Shadow prices interact with the distributional structure of final demand across income groups and member states. Higher shadow costs $\tau_j$ on ecosystem-intensive consumption goods (food, energy, tourism) are regressive if lower-income households spend larger shares of income on these categories. We report the distributional incidence of full shadow cost pass-through using Eurostat Household Budget Survey data, decomposing the implied price increase by income decile and member state. This analysis is deferred to @sec-results once the empirical estimates are available.

---

# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

This paper has developed a systemic framework for computing shadow prices of ecosystem services at NUTS2 regional resolution across the European Union. Our central theoretical result — the Leontief price dual with a biophysical satellite account — establishes that equilibrium shadow prices equal the marginal abatement costs of restoring degraded ecosystem assets to their SEEA EA baseline stocks (Proposition 1). Supply-chain amplification means that these prices, when embedded in the full input-output system, are uniformly higher than direct-attribution estimates (Proposition 2), and that uncorrected market equilibria generate self-reinforcing under-valuation as restoration costs escalate with cumulative deficits (Proposition 3).

The framework connects three literatures that have remained largely separate: environmental IO analysis, SEEA EA ecosystem accounting, and SFC macroeconomics. The SFC closure is essential: without it, ecosystem asset depletion shows up only in satellite accounts and leaves the financial system unchanged — an inconsistency that understates the balance-sheet risk of natural capital loss for sovereign and banking sectors. Our interest rate channel translates ecosystem degradation into an endogenous risk premium that feeds back through investment demand and gross output, creating a channel through which ecosystem pricing generates macroeconomic stabilization benefits beyond the direct Pigouvian correction.

Three policy implications follow directly. First, **ecosystem border adjustments** — the generalization of CBAM to all ecosystem service flows (Corollary 1) — are necessary alongside domestic pricing to prevent ecosystem leakage. Second, **ecological fiscal transfers** can be calibrated directly from the transboundary shadow cost matrix, providing a principled alternative to biophysical weighting schemes in current EFT proposals. Third, **natural capital adjusted NDP** that deducts the shadow cost of sustainability deficits from conventional income measures reveals that official statistics have systematically overstated welfare in member states with persistent ecosystem degradation.

The empirical implementation, using FIGARO-REG 2017 (10 macro-sectors, 288 NUTS2 regions, 29 countries) and INCA ecosystem service accounts (9 ES types, 2018), is described in @sec-estimation. Numerical results and welfare analysis are reported in @sec-results and @sec-welfare. We view this paper as a first step toward a full dynamic calibration of the model, which would require consistent time series of FIGARO-REG, INCA use tables, and restoration cost data — a data infrastructure that is now largely in place within EU statistical systems but not yet integrated into a unified shadow pricing framework.

---

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
